ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Yong Tau Foo exists; closest parent is tofu-based food product (FOODON_03311737).

serving.description: 8 pieces of Yong Tau Foo with anchovy soup
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving consists of 8 pieces of assorted Yong Tau Foo items (approximately 400 g total including soup broth), typically comprising stuffed tofu, bitter gourd, eggplant, okra, and chilli filled with fish paste, served in a clear anchovy-based broth.

nutrient_source.title: Yong Tau Foo (mixed pieces with clear soup) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for Yong Tau Foo dishes in Singapore context. Values per 100 g are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for Yong Tau Foo with clear soup and anchovy broth, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central data for fish paste, tofu, and anchovy broth components. The dish is a mixed plate; per-100 g values represent the composite edible portion including broth.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed tofu and vegetable dish with clear soup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yong Tau Foo with anchovy soup. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous mixed tofu-vegetable soup dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Tofu has a GI of approximately 15 (Atkinson et al., 2008, AJCN); mixed vegetable and fish-paste components contribute moderate carbohydrate with low GI. Composite GI of 40 is a conservative estimate consistent with the low-GI profile of tofu-dominant dishes. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 23.2) / 100 = 9.3.

hypertension.guidance: The anchovy-based soup broth contributes approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit broth consumption and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full 400 g serving may deliver around 1240 mg sodium, approaching the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yong Tau Foo with anchovy soup has an estimated low GI of 40 and a glycemic load of approximately 9.3 per serving, making it a suitable meal choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI noodle or rice additions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium from anchovy broth and moderate saturated fat from fish paste
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the dish provides lean protein from fish paste and tofu with relatively low saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g), the sodium-rich anchovy broth warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; consuming the solid pieces with minimal broth is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yong Tau Foo (酿豆腐) with anchovy soup is a popular Hakka Chinese-origin dish widely consumed in Malaysia and Singapore. The dish consists of 8 pieces of assorted items — typically including tofu, bitter gourd, eggplant, lady's finger (okra), and chilli — each stuffed with a seasoned fish paste mixture, then simmered or served in a clear, flavourful broth made from dried anchovies (ikan bilis). The texture of the pieces ranges from silky soft tofu to slightly firm vegetables, while the fish paste filling provides a springy, savoury bite. The anchovy broth is light, umami-rich, and mildly salty. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-protein, low-fat meal with a low glycemic index (~40), making it suitable for weight management and blood sugar control. The primary nutritional concerns are sodium from the broth and the need to monitor portion size when combined with noodles or rice. It is a halal-friendly, dairy-free dish commonly eaten as a main meal at hawker centres and food courts across Singapore and Malaysia. The dish is customisable, with diners selecting their preferred pieces from a display counter.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo entries for Yong Tau Foo with clear soup, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data. No single exact HPB entry for '8 pieces Yong Tau Foo with anchovy soup' was found; values represent a defensible composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on tofu-dominant mixed dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA component sources used for this dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary sources used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary sources used. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in fish-paste and tofu-based dishes per HPB and USDA data. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Yong Tau Foo with anchovy soup was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. GL per serving = (40 × 23.2 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 9.3. The cuisine tag 'Laksa' in the input is not applicable to this dish and has been disregarded. Price data (SGD 9.60) is preserved from the field survey source for reference only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
